coredata.dc:title: A novel label-free impedimetric immunosensor for sensitive detection of prostate specific antigen using Au nanoparticles/MWCNTs- graphene quantum dots nanocomposite
coredata.dc:creator: Ghanavati, Mehrnaz
Tadayon, Fariba
Bagheri, Hasan
coredata.dc:description: In this study, a novel impedimetric immunosensor based on Au nanoparticles@ multiwall carbon nanotubes-graphene quantum dots (AuNPs@MWCNTs-GQDs) was designed for the detection of prostate cancer biomarker with high sensitivity and selectivity in human serum. This label-free immunosensor was constructed by glassy carbon electrode surface coating with AuNPs@MWCNTs-GQDs as a signal amplifier and then an antibody against ،Prostate specific antigen)PSA(was immobilized on the modified electrode. The constructed immunosensor was characterized using transmission electron microscopy (TEM), Energy dispersive spectrometry (EDS), X-ray diffraction (XRD), Fourier transform infrared (FTIR), cyclic voltammetry (CV), and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) techniques. EIS measurements were applied to investigate the specific immuno-interactions at the immunosensor surface utilizing redox probe [Fe (CN)6]3−/4− to detect prostate specific antigen (PSA). The label-free immunosensor was successfully used to improve the analytical performance significantly and the impedance increment was linearly related to the PSA concentration in a wide linear range of 1–10,000 pg/mL and a low detection limit of 0.48 pg/mL. The responses of the proposed immunosensor in the real samples were compared with the commercial enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA) method. The obtained were in good agreement with those obtained by the reference ELISA method. Therefore, this sensor with high sensitivity, selectivity and repeatability, could be utilized for early clinical diagnosis of prostate cancer in the clinical laboratories.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Impedimetric immunosensor
Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy
Prostate-specific antigen
Label-free biosensors

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Optimization conditions of the antibody immobilization

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Baqiyatallah University of Medical Sciences and Farin behbood Tashkhis LTD

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors gratefully acknowledge the support of this work by Islamic Azad University, North Tehran Branch, Tehran, Iran. Also, The authors are thankful to the Research Council of Baqiyatallah University of Medical Sciences and Farin behbood Tashkhis LTD . They are grateful to Dr. Pegah Hashemi for her valuable suggestions.

The authors gratefully acknowledge the support of this work by Islamic Azad University, North Tehran Branch, Tehran, Iran. Also, The authors are thankful to the Research Council of Baqiyatallah University of Medical Sciences and Farin behbood Tashkhis LTD. They are grateful to Dr. Pegah Hashemi for her valuable suggestions.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) XRD patterns and (b) FTIR spectra of GQDs, MWCNTs, and AuNPs@MWCNTs-GQDs nanocomposite, (c) TEM images of GQDs and (d) AuNPs@MWCNTs-GQDs nanocomposite, (e) EDS elemental analysis spectrum of AuNPs@MWCNTs-GQDs nanocomposite.

a) DP voltammograms of the different electrodes, b) EIS and c) CV measurements for step by step fabrication of immunosensor. DPVs, CVs and EISs were recorded in PBS containing 5 mM of Fe[(CN)6]3−/4− and 0.1 M of KCl. For CV and EIS measurements, PSA concentration was 10 pg mL−1.
a) Effect of a) incubation concentration (40, 50, 70, 80, 100 and 150 μg/ml) and b) incubation time (15, 30, 40, 45, 60 and 90 min) of antibody.
Effect of incubation conditions of immunoreaction: a) pH, b) temperature and c) time (concentration of PSA is 10.0 pg/mL).
a) Nyquist plots and b) Calibration curve of impedimetric immunosensor (ΔRCT vs. log C) for PSA concentration. c) The specificity of the impedimetric immunosensor for the determination of PSA.
Randles parameters of fabrication step by step proposed immunosensor.
Comparison of the proposed immunosensor with the reported immunosensor for the determination of PSA.
Results of immunosensor performance in real samples.

head.ce:title: A novel label-free impedimetric immunosensor for sensitive detection of prostate specific antigen using Au nanoparticles/MWCNTs- graphene quantum dots nanocomposite

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Islamic Azad University, North Tehran Branch, Tehran, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Islamic Azad University, North Tehran Branch, Tehran, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Chemical Injuries Research Center, Systems Biology and Poisonings Institute, Baqiyatallah University of Medical Sciences, Tehran, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Chemical Injuries Research Center, Systems Biology and Poisonings Institute, Baqiyatallah University of Medical Sciences, Tehran, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, a novel impedimetric immunosensor based on Au nanoparticles@ multiwall carbon nanotubes-graphene quantum dots (AuNPs@MWCNTs-GQDs) was designed for the detection of prostate cancer biomarker with high sensitivity and selectivity in human serum. This label-free immunosensor was constructed by glassy carbon electrode surface coating with AuNPs@MWCNTs-GQDs as a signal amplifier and then an antibody against ،Prostate specific antigen)PSA(was immobilized on the modified electrode. The constructed immunosensor was characterized using transmission electron microscopy (TEM), Energy dispersive spectrometry (EDS), X-ray diffraction (XRD), Fourier transform infrared (FTIR), cyclic voltammetry (CV), and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) techniques. EIS measurements were applied to investigate the specific immuno-interactions at the immunosensor surface utilizing redox probe [Fe (CN)6]3−/4− to detect prostate specific antigen (PSA). The label-free immunosensor was successfully used to improve the analytical performance significantly and the impedance increment was linearly related to the PSA concentration in a wide linear range of 1–10,000 pg/mL and a low detection limit of 0.48 pg/mL. The responses of the proposed immunosensor in the real samples were compared with the commercial enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA) method. The obtained were in good agreement with those obtained by the reference ELISA method. Therefore, this sensor with high sensitivity, selectivity and repeatability, could be utilized for early clinical diagnosis of prostate cancer in the clinical laboratories.

ce:section.ce:para: Prostate specific antigen (PSA), a protein produced by both healthy prostate cells and prostate cancer cells, is composed of a 32–33 kDa single-chain glycoprotein, which was first identified in seminal plasma [1] and subsequently detached from the prostate tissue [2]. At present, PSA is considered to be the best biomarker in serum for the diagnosis of prostate cancer [3]. The concentration of PSA in the serum of healthy men is very low ranging from 0.1 to 4 ng/mL. When prostate concentrations are higher than normal, it is usually an indication of trauma or prostate disease [4]. If prostate cancer spreads beyond the limits of an organ, the probability of treatment decreases. Therefore, the development of methods for early and sensitive detection of prostate cancer to reduce mortality is essential.

Several methods have been developed for the diagnosis of prostate cancer, such as enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay [5] surface plasmon resonance [6], surface-enhanced Raman scattering [7], fluorescence [8,9] and electrochemical technologies [10,11]. Although most of these methods have high sensitivity, they have their limitations; for example, they are complicated, expensive, time-consuming, and require sample pretreatments [12]. Over the past two decades, biosensors based on electrochemical detection have revolutionized the point-of-care detection techniques as they are capable of producing quick, real-time, and accurate results in various places [10,11,13,14]. Electrochemical biosensors have been proposed as a valuable alternative to other methods of PSA detection [15–18].

EIS biosensors are a category of electrochemical methods which were introduced as a powerful label-free tool to investigate the interactions between the target and biorecognition element. The concentration of the target in solution is determined by measuring the number of bound biomolecules on the surface-immobilized bioreceptors, which causes changes in capacitance and/or resistance [19,20]. On the other hand, changes in the conductance of the electrode can be measured as a result of protein immobilization and antibody-antigen reactions on the electrode surface [21,22]. Therefore, the amplification of measurable signals using nanomaterials to modify the electrode surface not only leads to an increase in electron transport speed, but also enhances the adsorption capacity of the electrode surface for bioactive substances. Nanomaterials mostly used to modify electrodes of electrochemical immunosensors in recent years include metal nanoparticles, carbon nanomaterials, and quantum dots [23,24].

Noble metal nanoparticles, especially gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) have played an essential role in the development of label-free immunosensor. AuNPs accelerate direct electron transfer between redox probes and electrode surfaces as well as the stable immobilization of biomolecules while retaining their bioactivity [25,26].

In this paper, we designed a novel label-free electrochemical immunosensor based on the GCE surface modified with AuNPs@MWCNTs-GQDs nanocomposite for highly selective and sensitive detection of PSA by EIS. Using three compounds of AuNPs, MWCNT, and QOD lead to generating a novel nanocomposite that increases the sensitivity of label-free PSA immunosensor.

PSA, carcinoembryonic antigen (CEA) and alpha fetal protein (AFP) were acquired from Abcam and Beijing Dingguo Changsheng Biotechnology Co., Ltd. (Beijing, China), respectively. AuNP suspension, MWCNTs, citric acid, N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS), 1-Ethyl-3-(3-dimethyl aminopropyl) carbodiimide (EDC) and bovine serum albumin (BSA) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich Company. Phosphate buffer saline (PBS) contained NaCl, KCl and KH2PO4, Na2HPO4·2H2O were used. The pH of the buffer was adjusted with diluted solutions of NaOH or HCl. All other materials were analytical grade and purchased from Merck Company. Deionized water was used for the preparation of aqueous solutions.

All electrochemical experiments were carried out using an Ivium potentiostat/galvanostat (Vertex, Ivium Technologies, and The Netherlands). The electrochemical cell has consisted of the modified GCE as the working electrode, and Ag/AgCl and platinum wire were applied as the reference and counter electrodes, respectively. The X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were measured by Philips X-pert X-ray diffractometer and the radiation source was Cu Kα radiation (wavelength, λ = 1.5418 Å). The FT-IR spectra of the synthesized nanocomposite were recorded by a Shimadzu-8400S spectrometer. The UV–Vis spectrum and TEM images from AuNPs@MWCNTs-GQDs were obtained by a single beam UV-mini-WPA spectrophotometer and TEM (PHILIPS CM30), respectively. Also, the EDS was performed by an energy dispersive X-ray (EDX) Zeiss Evo 50.

GQDs were prepared through direct pyrolysis of citric acid, similar to the previous report [27]. Typically, 2.0 g citric acid was transferred into a 10 mL beaker and heated to 200 °C using a heating mantle until it was liquated and the color of the transparent liquid turned yellow. After 30 min, an orange liquid was obtained, which was afterward added dropwise to 100 mL of prepared NaOH solution (10 g/1 L) while the solution was stirred. Finally, the GQDs suspension neutralized to pH 7.0 by diluted hydrochloric acid

2.0 mL of AuNPs and 1 mL of GQDs suspension was mixed and then was added to 1 mg of acid-treated MWCNT under stirring for 12 h. The mixture sample was centrifuged for 30 min at 1400 rpm until unbound particles were removed. The supernatant was washed three times with distilled water and dried overnight in an oven at 35 °C. Then, 1 mg of synthesized nanocomposite was dispersed in 1 mL deionized water under sonication to obtain a homogeneous suspension. The suspension of AuNPs@MWCNTs-GQDs nanocomposite was stored at 4 °C in the dark until further usage.

The bare GCE was polished with 3.0 and 0.05 µm alumina powders and was sonicated in distilled water and ethanol for 5 min, respectively. After being washed thoroughly with deionized water, the electrode was dried at room temperature. After this step, 10 μL of prepared AuNPs@MWCNTs-GQDs was dropped on the clean GCE surface and was placed at room temperature to dry. Then, the AuNPs@MWCNTs-GQDs/GCE was immersed in a 5 mL mixture of 20 mM EDC and 10 mM NHS for 2 h at room temperature under stringing to activate the carboxylate group of the nanocomposite. Subsequently, the modified electrode was rinsed with PBS pH = 7 and 10 μL of antibody (100 µg/mL) was dropped on the electrode surface and was incubated at 4 °C for 45 min. At this stage, the prepared electrode was rinsed with deionized water to remove antibodies that had been physically adsorbed. To block nonspecific binding sites of the electrode, it was incubated in the bovine serum album (BSA) solution (0.05% w/v) for 1 h and washed subsequently. The preparation steps of the immunosensor were shown in Scheme 1 .

The applicability of the designed immunosensor to the human serum sample was investigated. Two serum samples obtained from healthy volunteers were collected and stored at 4° C temperature. The blood sample collected without any anticoagulant was allowed to clot overnight at 4° C and the serum was removed by centrifuged twice at 6000 rpm for 20 min each time. The obtained serum samples were frozen at −20° C for later analysis. Then, serum samples were spiked with two different concentrations of PSA so that the final concentration was within the range of calibration curve, and finally, the spiked and non-spiked samples were analyzed with proposed immunosensor.

The structure of the synthesized nanocomposite was investigated using XRD, FTIR, TEM and EDS techniques. XRD analysis was used to investigate the crystalline structure. Fig. 1 a indicates the XRD pattern of the samples. For the GQD sample, the located diffraction peak at 2ϴ = 24.2 corresponded to graphite crystallites and was indexed to (0 0 2) plane [28]. In MWCNTs pattern, two broad peaks were placed at 2θ = 25.7° and 43.2°, which referred to (0 0 2) and (1 1 0) crystalline planes, respectively, verifying the structure of MWCNTs [29]. After modification by Au, the new diffraction appeared at 2θ = 38, 44, 64 and 78 that indexed to (1 1 1), (2 0 0), (2 2 0) and (3 1 1) plane of Au nanoparticles respectively with face-centered cubic fcc structure [30]. Indeed, all peaks belonging to GQD and MWCNTs were preserved, which indicated that introducing Au nanoparticle did not destroy carbon materials.

FT-IR analysis was used to characterize the functional groups (i.e., OH and COOH) of GQDs, MWCNTs and Au/MWCNTs/GQDs. The spectra of GQD (Fig. 1b) show the five characteristic peaks such as CO stretching vibration at 1556 cm−1, C-O stretching vibration at 1271 cm−1, CH2 asymmetric stretching vibration at 2984 cm−1, symmetric stretching vibration at 2978 cm−1 and the OH stretching vibration from the carboxylic acid group [31].

The pristine MWCNTs spectra revealed several bands at 3335, 1635, 1354 cm−1, which referred to OH, CC and CO stretching vibrations, respectively. After the incorporation of Au ions, the new band was placed at 400–600 cm−1 that corresponded to the interaction of the Au nanoparticles with functional groups of the carbon substrate. The simultaneous presence of bands belonging to GQD, MWCNTs and Au nanoparticle confirmed the successful formation of the nanocomposite.

Also, the TEM and EDS techniques were applied for the study of nanocomposite morphology. Fig. 1c shows the TEM image of GQDs that showed a small size of 7 nm and circular shape. Fig. 1d illustrates a TEM image of AuNPs@MWCNT-GQD nanocomposite, where AuNPs and GQDs are well dispersed in the MWCNTs. As shown in Fig. 1e, the EDS analysis confirmed the presence of Au, O and C at the chemical composition of the nanocomposite.

The electrochemical response of the GCEs modified with GQD, AuNPs, MWCNT, AuNPs@MWCNT and AuNPs@MWCNT-GQD nanocomposite were investigate using differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) via the probe of [Fe(CN)6]3−/4−. For this purpose, modified electrodes were individually placed in the electrochemical cell containing 0.1 M PBS (pH = 7.4), 0.1 M KCl and 5 mM [Fe(CN)6]3−/[Fe(CN)6]4− (1:1) as a redox probe and their response peaks are recorded. As can be seen in Fig. 2 a, the current response of GQD/GCE, AuNPs/GCE, and MWCNTs/GCE is higher than the bare GCE because modified electrodes have a higher surface area. Once AuNPs@MWCNTs is used as a modifier, the signal is amplified. The combination of AuNPs@MWCNTs and GQD led to the development of a novel nanocomposite with new features such as high catalytic activity. Thus, AuNPs@MWCNT-GQD/GCE with the maximum response could be used in future experiments.

EIS and CV is an effective method used to study the surface features of immunosensor [32] . In this technique, [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− was used as a redox-probe. Obtained Nyquist plots and cyclic voltammograms of different modification steps of the surface electrode are shown in Fig. 2b and c. The Randles equivalent circuit consists of several parameters contain solution resistance (Rs), constant phase element (CPE or Q), electron transfer resistance (RCT) that equals semicircle diameter of EIS and the Warburg impedance (W) [33]. The parameter values were presented in Table 1 . RCT controls the electron transfer kinetics of the redox probe at the electrode interface [34]. When a bio-molecule is immobilized/ adsorbed on the electrode surface, it retards the electron transfer rates between the redox probe and electrochemical double layer, and hence, increases the charge transfer resistance for the redox probe to access the electrochemical double-layer [35].

As can be seen in Fig. 2, the bare GCE showed a semicircle with RCT = 927 Ω and also, its CV showed peak separation 303 mV and anodic peak current 28.05 μA. The value of charge transfer of GCE modified with AuNPs@MWCNT-GQD nanocomposite (RCT = 630.6 Ω) decreased due to the large surface area of the nanocomposite that can be effectively increased the electrical conductivity and the current response of the electrode. This result was confirmed by CV measurement that the oxidation peak current of CV increased (38.97 μA) and peak separation decreased (140 mv).

In the next stage, after activation of AuNPs@MWCNT-GQD/GCE with the mixture solution of EDC and NHS, the voltammogram of redox probe showed an increase in peak separation (190 mV), and a decrease in oxidation current peak (33.65 μA) and also increased in the RCT (1570 Ω). Once antibody and BSA were immobilized on the electrode surface, the charge-transfer resistance was remarkably enhanced due to the formation of insulating protein layers on the surface GCE/AuNPs@MWCNTs-GQDs/EDC-NHS, preventing redox probe from reaching to the electrode surface and also, blocking any remaining free spaces on the electrode surface by BSA. Subsequently, the anodic peak current of GCE/AuNPs@MWCNTs-GQDs/EDC-NHS/Ab and GCE/AuNPs@MWCNTs-GQDs/EDC-NHS/Ab/BSA decreased and their peaks separation increased, which confirmed the immobilization of antibody and blocking of the free spaces on the immunosensors [36]. Additionally, the RCT further increased with the incubation of PSA, which suggests the immunosensors fabricated successfully [37]. Therefore, there is a linear relationship between the RCT value of the designed immunosensor and the concentration of the analyte. As the concentration of the analyte increases, diffusion of the probe to the surface electrode was limited and RCT of the immunosensor surface increased.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization conditions of the antibody immobilization
ce:section.ce:para: The optimization of antibody concentration and incubation time are important factors that influence the sensitivity of the proposed immunosensor [38] Therefore, the various concentrations of antibody were immobilized and the results was investigated (Fig. 3 a). The RCT gradually enhanced as the concentration increased until it tended to stabilize at the range of 100–150 µg/mL. As a result, the concentration of 100 µg/mL was chosen as optimal. The influence of incubation time on the RCT of immunosensor in a constant concentration of antibody (100 μg/mL) was examined (Fig. 3b). The value of RCT increased by increasing the incubation time and the maximum value was achieved at 45 min, which was selected as an optimal incubation time

The pH value of the solution, temperature and incubation time of antigens are important parameters for the analytical performance of immunosensor. Since the electrochemical activity of both the antigen and antibody and its immunocomplex depends on pH, the effect of pH on the immunosensors response was investigated [39]. The RCT values were studied in the range of pH from 6 to 9 with the concentration of antigen 100 pg/mL and antibody 100 µg/mL that the results are shown in Fig. 4 a. It was found that RCT increased with increasing pH to 7.8 and then decrease to 9.0. The maximum RCT value was obtained at pH of 7.4.

Fig. 4b shows the influence of incubation temperature within the range of 20–50 °C on impedance response. The RCT value increased with increasing temperature up to 37 °C and then decreased in temperatures above 37 ◦C due to the fact that an irreversible reaction to occur (i.e., denaturation of proteins) [40], which negatively affects the process. Thus, the temperature of 37 °C was selected.

At the optimized pH and temperature, the effect of immunoreaction time was investigated in the time range of 15–90 min. The RCT was enhanced with increasing incubation time and reached a plateau at 60 min (Fig. 4c). Longer incubation times would not result in a significant change in impedance response, which indicated the saturated formation of immunocomplex between PSA antibody and PSA. Therefore, 60 min was applied as an optimized immunoreaction time.

The reproducibility of the proposed label-free immunosensor was evaluated through an analysis of five immunosensors that were fabricated at the same conditions and at PSA concentration 1000 pg/mL. The relative standard deviation (RSD) was 2.9%, which confirmed the reproducibility of the immunosensor.

Another important feature of the proposed immunosensor is long-term stability. The response of immunosensor was investigated in a one-month period. The result showed that 97.1% and 90% of the initial resistances after ten and thirty days, respectively, indicating acceptable stability.

To investigate the selectivity of the fabricated immunosensor under the optimal conditions, the response of immunosensor to some possible interferences such as carcinoembryonic antigen (CEA), alpha fetal protein (AFP), and immunoglobulin G (IgG) were recorded and compared to the response of designed immunosensor. As shown in Fig. 5c, the impedance response of the fabricated immunosensor toward PSA is much higher than that of other compounds, indicating that the immunosensor has a unique recognition ability toward PSA and its specificity is acceptable.

For evaluating the analytical feasibility and practical applications of the label-free immunosensor for PSA, the recovery experiments were carried out for the assay of two concentrations (10 and 100 pg/mL of PSA) in the human serum sample by the standard addition method. Table 3 shows the good recoveries obtained in the range of 97–102%. The responses of the proposed immunosensor were compared with the commercial ELISA method [39], which are listed in Table 3. The obtained results were in good agreement with those obtained by the reference ELISA method. Therefore, the results indicate that the proposed immunosensor is sufficiently appropriate to achieve PSA detection in real samples and also offers a promising approach for clinical research and diagnostic applications.

In this study, we synthesized a nanocomposite of AuNPs@MWCNTs-GQDs for signal amplification and used it to fabricate a label-free electrochemical immunosensor for the assay of PSA. The label-free PSA immunosensor showed an excellent linear relationship between the impedance change and different concentrations of PSA from 1 to 10,000 pg/mL and a low detection limit of 0.48 pg/mL which were within the range required for clinical detection. Furthermore, the designed immunosensor exhibited satisfactory selectivity, reproducibility, and stability, providing a great promise in clinical applications.

Mehrnaz Ghanavati: Resources, Investigation. Fariba Tadayon: Supervision, Data curation, Writing - original draft, Visualization. Hasan Bagheri: Supervision, Conceptualization, Methodology, Validation, Writing - review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledge the support of this work by Islamic Azad University, North Tehran Branch, Tehran, Iran. Also, The authors are thankful to the Research Council of Baqiyatallah University of Medical Sciences and Farin behbood Tashkhis LTD. They are grateful to Dr. Pegah Hashemi for her valuable suggestions.
